elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate proximal descent methods, inspired by the minimizing movement scheme introduced by Jordan, Kinderlehrer and Otto, for optimizing entropy-regularized functionals on the Wasserstein space.
elements.sent-2411-002: We establish linear convergence under flat convexity assumptions, thereby relaxing the common reliance on geodesic convexity.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our analysis circumvents the need for discrete-time adaptations of the Evolution Variational Inequality (EVI).
elements.sent-2411-004: Instead, we leverage a uniform logarithmic Sobolev inequality (LSI) and the entropy ``sandwich" lemma, extending the analysis from \cite{Nitanda2022ConvexAO, chizat2022meanfield .}
elements.sent-2411-005: The major challenge in the proof via LSI is to show that the relative Fisher information $I(\cdot|\pi)$ is well-defined at every step of the scheme.
elements.sent-2411-006: Since the relative entropy is not Wasserstein differentiable, we prove that along the scheme the iterates belong to a certain class of Sobolev regularity, and hence the relative entropy $\operatorname{KL (\cdot|\pi)$ has a unique Wasserstein sub-gradient, and that the relative Fisher information is indeed finite.}
elements.sent-2411-007: We consider the problem of minimizing an entropy-regularized flat-convex function \begin{equation}\label{eq:mean-field-min-problem} \min_{\mu \in \mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb R^d)} F^{\sigma}(\mu), \text{ with } F^{\sigma}(\mu)\coloneqq F(\mu) + \sigma \operatorname{KL}(\mu|\pi),\end{equation} over the Wasserstein space $\left(\mathcal{P _2(\mathbb R^d), \mathcal{W _2\right)$, where $F: \mathcal{P _2(\mathbb R^d) \to \mathbb R$ is a function bounded below on $\mathcal{P _2(\mathbb R^d)$, i.e., $\inf_{\mu \in \mathcal{P _2(\mathbb R^d) F(\mu) > -\infty$, $\pi \in \mathcal{P _2(\mathbb{R ^d)$ is a reference probability measure, $\sigma > 0$ is a regularization parameter and $\operatorname{KL}$ is the KL-divergence (relative entropy).}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-008: Such optimization problems are motivated by many applications in data science and machine learning, including the task of training two-layer neural networks (NNs) in the mean-field regime \cite{nitanda2017stochasticparticlegradientdescent, Chizat2018OnTG, Mei2018AMF, Rotskoff2018TrainabilityAA,sirignano , generative modeling \cite{huang2024generativemodelingminimizingwasserstein2, arbel and reinforcement learning \cite{leahy,ruiyi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: In this work, we tackle \eqref{eq:mean-field-min-problem from the perspective of discrete-time stepping schemes by proposing the following Jordan--Kinderlehrer--Otto (JKO)-based optimization methods: proximal point, prox-linear and proximal gradient,\footnote{We maintain the terminology used for analogous methods in finite-dimensional optimization; see e.g. \cite{Drusvyatskiy2016EfficiencyOM,parikh .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Indeed, our naming convention is justified since the JKO step \eqref{eq:JKO can be viewed as a proximal operator on the Wasserstein space $\left(\mathcal{P _2(\mathbb R^d), \mathcal{W _2\right)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: For $\pi \propto e^{-U}$ with a sufficiently regular potential $U: \mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R}$ and $F = 0$, given a step-size $\tau > 0$ and starting from $\mu^0 \in \mathcal{P _2(\mathbb R^d)$, the JKO scheme, also called minimizing movement scheme or proximal descent in the Wasserstein space, was originally introduced in \cite{jko and constructs a sequence $\left(\mu^n\right)_{n \in \mathbb N \subset \mathcal{P _2(\mathbb R^d)$ by the update rule \begin{equation}\label{eq:JKO} \mu^{n+1} = \argmin_{\mu \in \mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb R^d)} \left\{\sigma \operatorname{KL}(\mu|\pi) + \frac{1}{2\tau}\mathcal{W}_2^2(\mu, \mu^n)\right\},\end{equation} where $\mathcal{W _2$ is the $L^2$-Wasserstein distance.}}}}}}
